narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and an LSAT score of 36—both solid but slightly below the typical averages of 3.21 and 36.70 seen among past students—the admissions model evaluated your profile against historical data on bar exam outcomes. It predicted a 68.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, a risk high enough to lead to the rejection of your law school application, as the university prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The three biggest factors pushing this prediction toward failure were your academic decile rankings from earlier years, followed by your family income bracket. Most influential was your Year 3 school decile of 3, well below the average of 5.53, which signaled to the model a notable dip in performance relative to peers and strongly nudged the prediction toward expecting bar exam struggles. Next, your Year 1 decile of 4, compared to the typical 5.71, reinforced concerns about consistency in your grades from the start. Your upper-middle family income, while not low, also contributed by slightly elevating the risk in the model's eyes, perhaps reflecting patterns where such backgrounds correlate with certain preparation challenges seen in past data. These elements outweighed positives like your full-time student status, which is common among 92.7% of applicants and didn't shift the balance much. In contrast to typical students who pass the bar, your lower deciles stand out as the key departures, as higher rankings there often predict success. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male applicant—many in similar demographics succeed with stronger early academics—but the model weighs these patterns heavily to protect the school's bar passage rates. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT or GPA improvement resources, and reapplying after demonstrating upward trends. Building a narrative of academic resilience through additional certifications or work experience could also help in future appeals. You're close in several areas, so focused improvements here could flip the script for your next application.
